description: This figure presents optical micrographs illustrating the microstructure evolution of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling at different reduction ratios: (a) 10%, (b) 30%, (c) 50%, (d) 70%, and (e) 90%. The coordinate system indicates the rolling direction (RD) and normal direction (ND). The images reveal a distinct two-stage deformation mechanism: Stage I (10%–50% reduction) displays typical low-energy dislocation structures (LEDS) of Taylor-lattice-type resulting from planar dislocation glide, where slip bands gradually refine with increasing strain, showing spacing reduction from approximately 231 nm to 64 nm. Stage II (70%–90% reduction) exhibits pronounced grain elongation along RD and extensive shear band formation that progressively erodes the matrix, accompanied by ultrafine grains within shear bands. Additionally, nano-scale deformation twins (average thickness ~5.1 nm at 50% reduction, coarsening to ~19.2 nm at 70%) and microbands (average width ~166 nm) emerge at higher reductions. These microstructural transformations support the transition from "Copper-type" to "Brass-type" rolling texture and explain the substantial work hardening and strength enhancement (yield strength increasing from ~570 MPa to ~2100 MPa) in this lightweight high-manganese steel.
